food_subgroup: Processed fish products — grilled fish paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for otak-otak; closest defensible match is fish cake (FOODON_03311737). Otak-otak is a spiced fish paste grilled in leaf wrapping, distinct from Western-style fish cake.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Fish products and fish preparations) used as the closest defensible broader category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for otak-otak.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for otak-otak or equivalent spiced fish paste product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of otak-otak is approximately one piece (50g), equivalent to one banana-leaf parcel as commonly sold at hawker stalls and markets in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Otak-Otak (Fish Cake, Grilled) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for grilled fish cake / otak-otak used as primary source. Values cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for traditional Malay otak-otak preparation. Input enriched_json values (confidence 0.9) are consistent with HPB/MyFCD ranges and have been retained where aligned. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk and mackerel fat composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (mackerel, coconut milk). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g set to -1 as detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for traditional charcoal-grilled otak-otak are not individually reported in HPB Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Fish and Seafood Products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for otak-otak found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 estimated by analogy with grilled fish cake and high-protein, low-carbohydrate fish preparations reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) Diabetes Care international GI tables. Low GI is consistent with high protein (14.5g/100g) and fat (9.8g/100g) content from mackerel and coconut milk, with minimal carbohydrate (4.5g/100g). Glycemic load per 50g serving = (25 × 4.5 × 0.5) / 100 = 0.56, rounded to 0.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 380mg per 100g; one 50g serving contributes approximately 190mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit otak-otak to one piece per meal and avoid pairing with other high-sodium condiments or dishes, as cumulative sodium intake may exceed the recommended 2000mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated GI ~25) and low carbohydrate content (4.5g/100g) with high protein (14.5g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Otak-otak is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its high protein and fat content from fish and coconut milk produce a low glycemic response; one to two pieces per serving is appropriate within a balanced meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Mackerel provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids; however, coconut milk contributes saturated fat (estimated 4.2g/100g) and sodium is moderately elevated at 380mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume otak-otak in moderation (one piece per serving) to benefit from fish-derived omega-3 fatty acids, but should monitor overall saturated fat and sodium intake given the coconut milk base and seasoning content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mackerel provides omega-3 fatty acids; turmeric and galangal contain anti-inflammatory compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Otak-otak may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of omega-3 fatty acids from mackerel and bioactive compounds in turmeric and galangal used in the spice paste.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Otak-Otak is a traditional Malay and Peranakan spiced fish paste dish popular across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. It is made by blending ground fish — most commonly mackerel — with coconut milk and a fragrant spice paste of chili, turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, and shallots. The mixture is wrapped in banana or attap palm leaves and grilled over a charcoal fire, which imparts a distinctive smoky aroma and causes the paste to set into a soft, custardy, slightly springy texture. The result is a savory, mildly spicy, and aromatic parcel with a rich umami depth from the fish and a subtle sweetness from the coconut milk. Nutritionally, otak-otak is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate food: per 100g it provides approximately 165 kcal, 14.5g protein, 9.8g fat, and only 4.5g carbohydrate, with a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 25. It is a good source of vitamin B12 (2.5mcg/100g), selenium (18.5mcg/100g), niacin (3.5mg/100g), and phosphorus (160mg/100g) from the fish base. Sodium is moderately elevated at 380mg/100g due to added salt and seasonings, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Otak-otak is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its low glycemic load and high protein content. It is commonly served as a snack, side dish, or street food accompaniment to rice meals, and is a staple at hawker centres and markets throughout Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: What fish is used in otak-otak?
What is the glycemic index of otak-otak?
Is otah the same as otak-otak?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD ranges for grilled fish paste products and have been retained from the input enriched_json (confidence 0.9). Saturated fat is estimated from mackerel and coconut milk composition per USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for otak-otak was found. Detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data are not individually reported in primary approved sources for this traditional preparation, hence set to -1.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans), cholesterol, and moisture data for traditional charcoal-grilled otak-otak are not individually reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central does not contain a direct otak-otak entry and these sub-fractions are not available for this composite traditional preparation from any approved source consulted. saturated_fat_g (4.2g/100g) is estimated from the known fat composition of mackerel and coconut milk per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15046 Atlantic mackerel; NDB 12117 coconut milk). GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on grilled fish cake and high-protein low-carbohydrate fish preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 Diabetes Care international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for otak-otak exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per 50g serving calculated as (25 × 4.5 × 0.5) / 100 = 0.56, rounded to 0.6. FoodOn match (FOODON_03311737, fish cake) is a close but not exact match; otak-otak is a leaf-wrapped grilled spiced fish paste distinct from Western fish cake. FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Fish products and fish preparations) is a broader category match; no specific FoodEx2 code for otak-otak exists. All core macros (protein_g 14.5, fat_g 9.8, carbohydrate_g 4.5) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a mackerel-coconut milk fish paste product; no zero-macro justification required.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized input JSON to RESULT template structure; added mandatory multilingual names (Chinese, Tamil, Hindi); added saturated_fat_g estimation; added health_context with all three required conditions; added ontology anchors with gap notes.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close'/'broader' to 'closest_parent'; ensured -1 fields documented in notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document the missing-data basis for all five -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g) with reason for unavailability and approved sources consulted, resolving G3 numeric hygiene gate failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
